Group_05_Analysis: Prevelance of Obesity in Scotland (2013-2016)

Group 5: Chloe Au Yeung, Xinyan Tian, Cameron Fraser, and Sarah Khan

Introduction

Data Overview

In this report, we will begin using logistic regression to model outcomes of interest that take one of two categorical values. Then, we will use model diagnostics to assess if this model is the most reliable and fits the data best. Then we will look at the DAProject.csv dataset which contains the socio-economic and lifestyle factors from the 2013 - 2016 Scottish Health Surveys.

In this report, we will use the data stored in DAProject5.csv which has 7 variables and 14,018 individuals. DAPprojects.csv contains the following columns:

  • Age - Age of individual,

  • Sex - Sex of individual (Male/ Female),

  • Education - Highest educational qualification of individual,

  • Veg - Consume recommended daily vegetable intake (Yes / No),

  • Fruit - Consume recommended daily fruit intake (Yes / No),

  • Year - Year of the Scottish Health Survey,

  • Obese - Indicator of individuals obesity classification (Yes / No).

Since this dataset contains a binary categorical variable Obese - classified as Yes/No - logistic regression may be considered as an appropriate modelling approach. The main questions of interest are:

  • Has the prevalence of obesity in Scotland changed over the given years of the Scottish Health Survey?

  • Are there any differences in obesity by age, gender, socio-economic status, or lifestyle factors?

Explanatory Data Analysis

Logistic Regression

Logistic regression is used when the outcome of interest takes one of two categorical values (e.g. Yes/No). In this case, the response variable takes the value 1 (defined as success, with probability \(p_i\)) or 0 (defined as failure, with probability \(1 - p_i\)). And the response is given by \(Y_i\) with Bernoulli distribution. The corresponding model uses the logit link function: \[ g(p_i) = \log\left(\frac{p_i}{1 - p_i}\right) = \log\left(\frac{\mathbb{P}(Y_i = 1 \mid \underline{x}_i)}{\mathbb{P}(Y_i = 0 \mid \underline{x}_i)}\right) = \underline{x}_i^\top \underline{\beta} \tag{1}\]

We can refer to Equation 1 as the log-odds, since \(\frac{p_i}{1 - p_i}\) represents the odds. Taking the inverse of the logit link function gives the following: \[ \mathbb{P}(Y_i = 1 \mid \underline{x}_i) = \frac{\exp(\underline{x}_i^\top \underline{\beta})}{1 + \exp(\underline{x}_i^\top \underline{\beta})} \tag{2}\] \[ \mathbb{P}(Y_i = 0 \mid \underline{x}_i) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(\underline{x}_i^\top \underline{\beta})}. \tag{3}\]

Visualisations

To get an initial impression of the data set, we will look at the binary categorical variable Obese as our response variable.

Year vs prevalence

We can look at a bar plot of the proportion of Obese by Year to observe if there are changes in prevalence across the years.

We can see in Figure 1 that there was an approximate consistency in proportion of individuals in the Yes obesity group. From Table 1, the proportion of individuals in the Yes obesity group appears, on average, at 30.5%.

Sex vs prevalence

In Table 2, we can see that females in the Yes obesity group tends to be slightly higher than that of males.

Age vs prevalence

Figure 2 shows the distribution of age by obesity status. Individuals classified as obese tends to have slightly higher median ages compared to non-obese individuals.

Education vs prevalence

From Table 3, proportion of individuals in the Yes obesity group variably increases across education levels. For example, individuals in the No Qualifications group have a higher prevalence of obesity (38.2%), than those in the Degree of higher group (24.7%).

Vegetable Intake

Fruits Intake

Additionally, Table 4 and Table 5 displays similar results for nutritional consumption Yes/No with the Fruit group intake being approximately the same and the No Vegetable intake group having a larger proportion (32.6% vs 29.8%).

Formal Data Analysis

Model Specification

We will now fit a logistic model to investigate the relationship between obesity and explanatory variables. Let \(Y_i = 1\) if individual i is obese and \(Y_i = 0\) otherwise. We will assume that: \[Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)\]where, \(p_i = \mathbb{P}(Y_i = 1 \mid \underline{x}_i)\) and \(Y_i = Obese_i\). The model is given by: \[logit(p_i)=\alpha_0 + \beta_1 \,\mathbb{I}_{(\text{Year}_i = 2014)} + \beta_2 \,\mathbb{I}_{(\text{Year}_i = 2015)} + \beta_3 \,\mathbb{I}_{(\text{Year}_i = 2016)} \tag{4}\]

where: 2013 is the reference year. The intercept \(\alpha_0\) represents the log-odds of an individual being obese in the reference year 2013, and the coefficients \(\beta_1\), \(\beta_2\), \(\beta_3\) represent the change in log-odds in each year compared to 2013.

To fit a logistic regression model, we will conduct variable selection using a backwards elimination approach, supported by model selection critera such as Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) and likelihood ratio test. The performance of the model we select will be assessed using the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve.

Results

Year vs prevalence

We will fit a logistic regression model using the logit link function, with Obese as the response and Year as the explanatory variable:

The coefficients for all years appear close to zero. Additionally, the confidence intervals across all years include zero and p > 0.05. This provides no evidence that year is a statistically significant predictor of obesity, which suggests that obesity prevalence has not changed over time. We can visualise the probabilities using the plot_model() function as follows:

Figure 3 shows the estimated log-odds coefficients for each year relative to the reference category (2013) are all close to zero, and their confidence intervals include zero. This indicates that there is minimal difference in log-odds of obesity across years. Adding onto this, Table 6 shows large p-values, which further provides evidence that year is not a statistically significant predictor of obesity. Comparing coefficients of the regression model with that of the reference level appears to be approximately the same. We can now obtain probability predictions for each year using the predict() function as follows:

From Figure 4, we can predict the probability of obesity for each year. The probabilities appear to be constant at approximately 30%, with the confidence intervals overlapping. This, again, indicates that there is no significant change in obesity prevalence over time.

Comparison: Full Model vs. Reduced Model (with fruit intake removed)

We now investigate whether obesity differs by age, gender, socio-economic status, and lifestyle factors. By this, we will fit a full model including variables Age, Sex, Education, Veg, and Fruit. Our full model indicates that fruit intake is not a statistically significant predictor for obesity due to the large p-value (0.942) so it is removed. As seen in the reduced model, and to compare models we also fit a reduced model, with Fruit removed:

In summary, due to the insignificant p-value, we can remove fruit intake from the full model. The reduced model predictors are now all statistically significant. Next, we will compare the BIC of the reduced model again the full model to supplement this conclusion:

The full model has a higher BIC in comparison to the reduced model, indicating a poorer balance between model fit and complexity. As a result, the reduced model is preferred. In addition to this, we can conduct a likelihood ratio test:

A likelihood ratio test comparing the reduced and full models yields a p-value of 0.94. Since this is greater than 0.05, there is no evidence that the additional predictor, fruit intake, improve model fit.

Model check

We will now investigate diagnostic plots to assess how well the model fits the data. This way we can assess whether our assumptions are met:

The posterior predictive check (top-left) shows that most residuals lie within the expected error bounds, with no strong systematic pattern. This suggests there is no systematic discrepancies between real and simulated data. The binned residual plot (top-right) shows that most of the residuals lie within the expected error bounds, with about 95% of the residuals to fall inside the error bounds. The influential observations plot (bottom-left) indicates that all points on this plot lie within Cook’s distance. Therefore, there is no evidence of influential observations that affect the model. The uniformity of residuals (bottom-right) closely follows the theoretical line, suggesting that the model assumptions are appropriate and to which the observed values do not deviate from the model expectations using simulated residuals.

Finally, we will assess the performance of the model using the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve:

The ROC curve in Figure 5 indicates if the model was able to distinguish between obese and non-obese individuals. Since the curve appears to be close to the diagonal line, this indicates the model only performs slightly better than guessing at random. The area under the curve (AUC) is approximately 0.60, which suggests a moderate-poor predictive performance. This indicates that, although several predictors are statistically significant, the effect they have are not strong.

Discussion

There is no significant evidence to suggest that obesity rates in the Scottish population change from 2013-2016. Table 6 shows the proportions as relatively similar across years, and this is further evidenced by the logistic regression model used to predict obesity, as Figure 3 shows confidence intervals that include 0, meaning there is very little difference in the log-odds across the years. There are differences in obesity by age, gender, socio-economic status, or lifestyle factors. This is evidenced by Table 2, Figure 2, Table 3, Table 4, as all of these show a difference in obesity prevalence within their own category. Furthermore, by being significant predictors of obesity in the logistic regression model - Age, Sex, Education, and Veg must have significant differences in obesity prevalence within their categories, otherwise they would not be significant predictors, like Fruit.

Conclusion

In conclusion, there is no suggested evidence that obesity prevalence has any significant chance over the years we considered in this report. Moreover, there was evidence of a difference in obesity by age, gender, socio-economic status, or vegetable consumption, however, no evidence of a difference was observed by fruit.

Reference

Scottish Government (2013-2016). Scottish Health Survey.
